metadata.title: Verb To Be
learning_objectives: Use am, is, are correctly with subject pronouns
Form affirmative sentences with verb to be
Form negative sentences with verb to be
Ask questions using verb to be
Give short answers with verb to be
Use contractions naturally in speech and writing
Introduce yourself and ask for personal information
Describe people and things using verb to be

metadata.title: Verb To Be
learning_objectives: Use am, is, are correctly with subject pronouns
Form affirmative sentences with verb to be
Form negative sentences with verb to be
Ask questions using verb to be
Give short answers with verb to be
Use contractions naturally in speech and writing
Introduce yourself and ask for personal information
Describe people and things using verb to be

explanation.simple_explanation: The verb to be is the most important verb in English. It tells us who someone is, what something is, or how someone feels. In the present tense, it has three forms: am, is, and are. You choose the correct form based on the subject of the sentence.
when_to_use: To introduce yourself and others: I am Ahmed.
To say your nationality: She is Egyptian.
To describe age: He is ten years old.
To describe feelings: They are happy.
To describe physical appearance: It is big.
To talk about jobs: I am a teacher.
To give personal information: My name is Sara.
To talk about location: The book is on the table.
To identify people and things: This is my friend.
To talk about the time: It is 3 o'clock.
To talk about the weather: It is cold today.
To describe relationships: He is my brother.

sentence_structure.affirmative: Subject + am/is/are + complement. Example: I am a student.
sentence_structure.negative: Subject + am/is/are + not + complement. Example: She is not a teacher.
sentence_structure.question: Am/Is/Are + subject + complement? Example: Are you happy?
sentence_structure.short_answer_affirmative: Yes, + subject pronoun + am/is/are. Example: Yes, I am.
sentence_structure.short_answer_negative: No, + subject pronoun + am/is/are + not. Example: No, she is not.

common_patterns: Subject + be + noun: I am a doctor.
Subject + be + adjective: They are tall.
Subject + be + location: The pen is on the desk.
Subject + be + age: He is twenty years old.
Subject + be + nationality: We are Jordanian.
Subject + be + feeling: She is tired.
This/That + be + noun: This is a book.
There + be + noun: There is a cat in the room.

affirmative.english: The cat is on the chair.

affirmative.grammar_note: Use 'am from' to state origin with 'I'.

negative.grammar_note: Add 'not' after 'am' to make it negative.
Use 'is not' to express the opposite.
Use 'is not' with a singular noun.
Use 'is not' to negate age status.

negative.english: The books are not on the desk.
negative.grammar_note: Use 'are not' with a plural noun.

questions.grammar_note: Start with 'am' for questions with 'I'.
Start with 'are' for questions with 'you'.
Start with 'is' for questions with 'he'.
Start with 'is' for questions with 'she'.
Start with 'is' for questions with 'it'.
Start with 'are' for questions with 'we'.
Start with 'are' for questions with 'they'.
Use question word + 'is' with 'what'.
Use 'where are' to ask about origin.
Use 'how is' to ask about condition.
Use 'is' with 'this' in questions.
Use 'are you' to ask about feelings.
Use 'is she' to ask about relationships.
Use 'what is' to ask about objects.
Use 'who is' to ask about identity.
Use 'how old are' to ask about age.

questions.english: Are the books on the table?
questions.grammar_note: Use 'are' with plural nouns in questions.
Use 'is it' to ask about time.
Use 'are' with compound subjects.
Use 'what color is' for specific questions.

short_answers.grammar_note: Negative short answer with 'I'. Cannot use contraction 'amn't'.
Short answer can include extra information.

short_answers.english: No, he is not my father.
No, we are not ready yet.

common_mistakes.reason: Use 'is' with 'he', not 'am'. 'Am' is only used with the subject 'I'.
Use 'is' with 'she', not 'are'. 'Are' is used with you, we, they.
Use 'are' with 'we', not 'is'. 'Is' is only for he, she, it and singular nouns.
The word 'no' does not create negatives in English. Use 'not' after the verb.
Place 'not' after the verb 'are', not before it.
Do not repeat the verb. Use one form of 'be' in the question.
Do not add 'is' after 'am' in short answers. Use only the correct form once.
The negative form must follow the pattern: subject + verb + not. Do not place 'not' before the verb.
Question words (where, what, who) come at the beginning of the question in English.
Use the article 'a' or 'an' before singular countable nouns when describing someone's occupation or identity.
Singular countable nouns need an article (a/an) after the verb 'to be'.
Use 'are' with 'they' because 'they' is plural. 'Is' is only for singular subjects.
There is no contraction for 'am not' in standard English. Use the full form 'am not'.
The short answer for 'I' must use 'am', not 'are'. Each pronoun has its own matching form.

common_mistakes.correct: No, he is not a doctor.
common_mistakes.reason: In short answers, use the full negative form. 'Isn't' is acceptable in speech but the full form is preferred in writing.
Use the possessive adjective 'your', not the subject pronoun 'you', before the noun 'name'.
Use 'is' with 'it'. 'It' is a singular subject pronoun.

mini_rules.rule: Use 'am' only with the subject 'I'.
mini_rules.example: I am happy.

mini_rules.rule: Use 'is' with 'he', 'she', and 'it'.
mini_rules.example: He is tall. She is kind. It is big.

mini_rules.rule: Use 'are' with 'you', 'we', and 'they'.
mini_rules.example: You are smart. We are ready. They are here.

mini_rules.rule: Use 'are' with singular 'you' and plural 'you'.
mini_rules.example: You are a friend. You are friends.

mini_rules.rule: Use 'a' or 'an' before singular nouns after 'be'.
mini_rules.example: I am a teacher. She is an engineer.

mini_rules.rule: Place 'not' after am, is, or are to make negatives.
mini_rules.example: It is not cold.

mini_rules.rule: Invert the subject and verb to form yes/no questions.
mini_rules.example: Is she your sister?

mini_rules.rule: Question words come first in information questions.
mini_rules.example: Where are you from?

mini_rules.rule: Short affirmative answers use 'Yes' + comma + subject + verb.
mini_rules.example: Yes, I am.

mini_rules.rule: Short negative answers use 'No' + comma + subject + verb + not.
mini_rules.example: No, they are not.

mini_rules.rule: Contractions combine subject and verb: I am = I'm.
mini_rules.example: I'm a student.

mini_rules.rule: Contractions combine verb and not: is not = isn't.
mini_rules.example: She isn't here.

mini_rules.rule: There is no contraction for 'am not' (amn't is not standard).
mini_rules.example: I am not late. (Not: I amn't late)

mini_rules.rule: Use 'it is' for time, weather, and distance.
mini_rules.example: It is 5 o'clock. It is sunny. It is far.

mini_rules.rule: Use 'there is' with singular nouns and 'there are' with plural nouns.
mini_rules.example: There is a book. There are books.

mini_rules.rule: Do not use double subjects like 'My brother he is tall'.
mini_rules.example: My brother is tall. (Not: My brother he is tall)

mini_rules.rule: Use 'this is' for something near and 'that is' for something far.
mini_rules.example: This is my phone. That is your bag.

mini_rules.rule: Adjectives after 'be' have no plural form.
mini_rules.example: They are happy. (Not: They are happies)

mini_rules.rule: Use 'how old' to ask about age with 'be'.
mini_rules.example: How old are you?

mini_rules.rule: Use 'who is' to ask about identity and 'what is' to ask about things.
mini_rules.example: Who is she? What is this?

reading_connections.situation: A short biography: 'She is Maria. She is from Spain. She is 25 years old. She is a doctor.'
reading_connections.focus: Using 'is' with she for personal information.

reading_connections.situation: A family introduction: 'This is my family. My father is tall. My mother is kind. We are happy.'
reading_connections.focus: Using 'is' and 'are' to describe family members.

reading_connections.situation: A classroom poster: 'I am a student. This is my school. It is big.'
reading_connections.focus: Using 'am' with I and 'is' with it.

reading_connections.situation: A simple email: 'Hi! I am Tom. I am from London. I am a teacher.'
A lost item notice: 'This is a blue bag. It is on the desk.'
reading_connections.focus: Using 'is' with this and it for objects.

reading_connections.situation: A weather report: 'Today is sunny. It is hot. The sky is blue.'
A social media profile: 'Hi! I am Sara. I am 20. I am from Egypt. My favorite color is green.'
A simple dialogue in a textbook: 'A: Are you a student? B: Yes, I am.'
reading_connections.focus: Reading question and short answer patterns.

reading_connections.situation: A description of friends: 'Ali and Omar are friends. They are in the park. They are happy.'
A classroom rules poster: 'We are kind. We are ready. We are on time.'
reading_connections.focus: Using 'are' with we for group identity.

listening_connections.situation: Hearing someone introduce themselves at a meeting: 'Hello, I am Mr. Smith. I am your teacher.'
Listening to a receptionist ask: 'What is your name? Where are you from?'
listening_connections.focus: Recognizing question forms with is and are.

listening_connections.situation: Hearing a friend say: 'She is my sister. She is 15 years old.'
Listening to a weather forecast: 'It is rainy today. The temperature is 20 degrees.'
Hearing a teacher ask the class: 'Are you ready? Is everyone here?'
listening_connections.focus: Recognizing questions with are and is.

listening_connections.situation: Listening to a guided tour: 'This is the museum. It is very old.'
Hearing airport announcements: 'Flight 305 is delayed. The gate is number 12.'
Listening to a phone conversation: 'Hi, is Ali there? No, he is not.'
Hearing a song for children: 'You are my sunshine, my only sunshine.'
Listening to a friend describe people in a photo: 'This is my mother. She is kind. They are my cousins. They are funny.'
listening_connections.focus: Recognizing mixed forms of be in natural speech.

speaking_connections.scenario: Introduce yourself to a new classmate. Say your name, age, and country.
Ask a partner for their name and where they are from.
Describe your family members using he/she and is.
speaking_connections.focus: Using 'he is' and 'she is' for describing people.

speaking_connections.scenario: Answer yes/no questions about yourself: 'Are you a student? Are you 20?'
speaking_connections.focus: Practicing short answers: Yes, I am. No, I am not.

speaking_connections.scenario: Describe objects in your bag using colors: 'It is blue. It is small.'
Talk about your classmates: 'He is tall. She is kind. They are friendly.'
Ask and answer about feelings: 'Are you happy? Yes, I am.'
Role-play a receptionist and guest: 'What is your name? Where are you from?'
speaking_connections.focus: Using question forms in a service context.

speaking_connections.scenario: Describe where things are in the classroom: 'The book is on the desk. The pen is in the bag.'
Introduce two friends to each other: 'This is Ali. He is from Jordan. This is Sara. She is from Egypt.'
speaking_connections.focus: Using 'this is' for introductions and 'is' for origin.

ai_conversation_instructions.grammar_focus: Verb to be in present tense: am, is, are
required_corrections: Ensure proper question formation (inversion of subject and verb)
Ensure 'not' placement is after the verb in negatives
Ensure short answers use the correct pronoun and verb
Correct any missing articles (a/an) before singular count nouns after be
common_errors_to_watch: Placing 'not' before the verb instead of after
Using 'no' instead of 'not' to form negatives
Forgetting to invert in questions (e.g., You are happy? instead of Are you happy?)
Adding extra verbs in short answers (e.g., Yes, I am is)
Missing articles before nouns (e.g., I am student instead of I am a student)
Using 'amn't' which is not standard English
conversation_goals: Student can introduce themselves naturally using I am
Student can ask for personal information using What is, Where are, How old are
Student can answer yes/no questions with correct short answers
Student can describe people and things using he/she/it/they + is/are
Student can use contractions in speech for natural fluency
Student can form negative statements about personal information

multiple_choice.question: What ___ your name?
multiple_choice.explanation: Use 'is' with 'what' to ask for a name.

multiple_choice.question: Where ___ you from?
multiple_choice.explanation: Use 'are' with 'you' in questions about origin.

multiple_choice.question: I ___ not tired.
multiple_choice.explanation: Use 'am not' with 'I' in negative sentences.

multiple_choice.question: How old ___ you?
multiple_choice.explanation: Use 'are' with 'you' to ask about age.

multiple_choice.question: He ___ a doctor.
multiple_choice.explanation: Use 'is' with the subject 'he' for occupations.

multiple_choice.question: Who ___ he?
multiple_choice.explanation: Use 'is' with 'who' to ask about identity.

sentence_ordering.correct_sentence: The cat is on the chair.

error_correction.explanation: Use 'not' after the verb, not 'no' before it.
Use 'where' at the start and 'are' with 'you'.
Do not repeat 'is' in the question.
Short answers use only one verb form.

error_correction.wrong_sentence: The books is on the desk.
error_correction.correct_sentence: The books are on the desk.

error_correction.explanation: There is no contraction 'amn't' in standard English.
Do not use a double subject (my mother + she).

transformation.instruction: Change to negative: 'I am a student.'
Change to question: 'You are happy.'
Change to affirmative: 'She is not a teacher.'
Change to use contraction: 'I am from Egypt.'
Change to question: 'They are from Jordan.'
Change to negative: 'He is a doctor.'
Change to use contraction: 'She is not here.'
Change to affirmative: 'We are not friends.'
Change to question: 'It is cold today.'
Change to negative: 'They are at home.'
Change to short answer affirmative: 'Are you a student?'
transformation.hint: Start with 'Yes', add comma, then pronoun + verb.

transformation.instruction: Change to short answer negative: 'Is she your sister?'
transformation.hint: Start with 'No', add comma, then pronoun + verb + not.

transformation.instruction: Change to question with 'where': 'You are from Cairo.'
Change to use contraction: 'They are not ready.'
Change to negative: 'I am a teacher.'
Change to question with 'what': 'This is a book.'
Change to affirmative: 'He is not my brother.'
Change to question: 'She is a nurse.'
Change to use contraction: 'We are happy.'
Change to negative: 'You are tall.'

translation.expected_english: The cat is on the chair.
The books are not on the desk.
No, they are not at home.

true_or_false.statement: We use 'am' with the subject 'I'.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. 'Am' is only used with 'I'.

true_or_false.statement: We use 'are' with the subject 'he'.
true_or_false.explanation: We use 'is' with 'he', not 'are'.

true_or_false.statement: 'She is not' is a negative sentence.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. 'Is not' makes the sentence negative.

true_or_false.statement: In questions, we put the verb before the subject.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. For example: 'Are you ready?'

true_or_false.statement: We use 'is' with 'they'.
true_or_false.explanation: We use 'are' with 'they'.

true_or_false.statement: 'I amn't' is correct English.
true_or_false.explanation: There is no contraction for 'am not'.

true_or_false.statement: 'Yes, I am' is a short answer.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. It is an affirmative short answer.

true_or_false.statement: We say 'I am a student' without 'a'.
true_or_false.explanation: We need 'a' before 'student': 'I am a student.'

true_or_false.statement: 'What is your name?' is a correct question.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. It uses 'is' with 'what'.

true_or_false.statement: 'We is happy' is correct.
true_or_false.explanation: We should say 'We are happy'.

true_or_false.statement: We use 'are' with 'you' in questions and statements.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. 'You are' and 'Are you?'

true_or_false.statement: 'I not am tired' is correct negative form.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct form is 'I am not tired'.

true_or_false.statement: 'They are from Egypt' means the same as 'They from Egypt.'
true_or_false.explanation: The verb 'are' is necessary. 'They from Egypt' is not correct.

true_or_false.statement: We can say 'it's' instead of 'it is'.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. 'It's' is the contraction of 'it is'.

true_or_false.statement: In short answers, we can say 'Yes, I am is.'
true_or_false.explanation: We must say only 'Yes, I am.' without extra verb.

true_or_false.statement: 'She is a nurse' uses 'is' correctly.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. 'Is' goes with 'she'.

true_or_false.statement: 'How old are you?' asks about age.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. 'How old' is used for age questions.

true_or_false.statement: 'No, he isn't' is not a valid short answer.
true_or_false.explanation: It is valid. 'Isn't' is the contraction of 'is not'.

true_or_false.statement: We can use 'there is' with plural nouns.
true_or_false.explanation: We use 'there is' with singular and 'there are' with plural.

true_or_false.statement: The verb 'to be' is the most common verb in English.
true_or_false.explanation: Correct. It is the most fundamental and frequently used verb.

questions.question: Change to question: 'You are a teacher.'
Choose the correct short answer: 'Is she your sister?'
Correct this: 'I no am a student.'
Change to negative: 'She is a teacher.'

flashcards.front: What is the verb to be for 'I'?
What is the verb to be for 'he/she/it'?
What is the verb to be for 'you/we/they'?
How do you make a negative with 'to be'?
flashcards.back: Add 'not' after the verb: I am not, he is not, they are not

flashcards.front: How do you make a question with 'to be'?
flashcards.back: Invert the subject and verb: Am I? Is he? Are you?

flashcards.front: What is the contraction of 'I am'?
What is the contraction of 'she is'?
What is the contraction of 'they are'?
What is the contraction of 'is not'?
What is the contraction of 'are not'?

flashcards.back: No. There is no contraction for 'am not'.

flashcards.front: How do you give a short affirmative answer?
flashcards.back: Yes, + subject pronoun + verb (e.g., Yes, I am.)

flashcards.front: How do you give a short negative answer?
flashcards.back: No, + subject pronoun + verb + not (e.g., No, she is not.)

flashcards.front: What does 'it is' describe in weather?
flashcards.back: Weather: It is hot. It is cold. It is sunny.

flashcards.front: How do you ask about age?
flashcards.back: How old + are/is + subject? (e.g., How old are you?)

key_rules: Always use 'a/an' before singular nouns after be

revision_questions.question: What are the three forms of the verb 'to be' in present tense?
How do you form a yes/no question with 'to be'?
revision_questions.answer: Put the verb (am/is/are) before the subject.

revision_questions.question: What is the rule for forming negatives with 'to be'?
revision_questions.answer: Add 'not' after the verb: am not, is not, are not.

revision_questions.question: What is different about 'am not' compared to 'is not' and 'are not'?
Why do we say 'I am a student' and not 'I am student'?
revision_questions.answer: Singular countable nouns need the article 'a' or 'an' after the verb to be.

spaced_repetition_notes.review_focus: Introduce the three forms: am, is, are. Practice with subject pronouns.
Practice affirmative sentences with all pronouns. Introduce contractions.
Practice negative sentences. Contrast with affirmative forms.
Practice yes/no questions and short answers.
Mix all forms: affirmative, negative, questions, short answers.
Extended practice with WH-questions (what, where, who, how).
Comprehensive review with real-world speaking and writing tasks.

completion_criteria: Correctly conjugate am/is/are with all subject pronouns in affirmative sentences
Form negatives by adding 'not' after the correct form of be
Form yes/no questions by inverting subject and verb correctly
Answer questions with appropriate short answers (affirmative and negative)
Use contractions (I'm, he's, she's, it's, you're, we're, they're, isn't, aren't)
Form WH-questions (what, where, who, how old, how) with correct word order
Score at least 80% on the final assessment quiz
Complete all 160 exercise items across 8 exercise types
Identify and correct common mistakes with verb to be
Use verb to be in at least 3 real-world speaking scenarios

mastery_criteria.definition: The learner has mastered the verb 'to be' at A1 level when they can independently and accurately use am, is, and are in all forms (affirmative, negative, questions, short answers) without conscious effort in familiar contexts.

level_1_beginner.description: Can identify am, is, are but makes frequent errors with subject-verb agreement.

level_2_developing.description: Can use am, is, are correctly in affirmative sentences but makes errors in negatives and questions.

level_3_proficient.description: Can use all forms correctly in controlled exercises but still makes occasional errors in spontaneous speech.

level_4_advanced.description: Can use all forms correctly in both controlled and spontaneous contexts with minimal errors.

level_5_mastered.description: Uses verb to be correctly and naturally in all forms, with contractions, in speaking and writing without hesitation.

previous_grammar.title: Basic Nouns and Pronouns
previous_grammar.reason: Learners need to know subject pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) before using verb to be.

next_grammar.title: Articles: A/An/The
next_grammar.reason: Learners need articles to correctly form noun phrases after verb to be (e.g., I am a student).

grammar_often_confused.reason: Learners often confuse verb to be conjugation with present simple conjugation of regular verbs (e.g., 'He is go' instead of 'He goes').
While related, 'there is/are' follows different word order and has a different function from subject + be.

grammar_often_confused.grammar: Verb to be in Past Tense (was/were)
grammar_often_confused.reason: When learners progress to past tense, they confuse was/were with am/is/are.

common_l1_interference_notes: Arabic speakers may drop the verb to be in present tense because Arabic does not use a copula in present tense equational sentences
Arabic speakers may confuse 'is' and 'are' because Arabic verb conjugation follows different patterns
Speakers of languages without articles may forget 'a/an' after verb to be
